test_cases.title: Successful login with valid email and password
steps: POST /api/auth/login with valid email and bcrypt-hashed password
Verify response sets auth_token cookie with httpOnly, secure, sameSite=lax, path=/ attributes
Verify cookie contains a 3-part token in userId.issuedAt.signature format
Verify response returns user profile data
test_cases.expected_result: User receives a valid 3-part auth_token cookie and is authenticated with a 200 response containing user profile

test_cases.title: Successful user registration
steps: POST /api/auth/register with valid name, email, and password
Verify password is hashed with bcrypt before storage
Verify a new user record is created in the database with companyId
Verify response sets auth_token cookie with 3-part token format
test_cases.expected_result: New user is created, password stored as bcrypt hash, and auth_token cookie is set for immediate session

test_cases.title: Token refresh extends session via middleware sliding window
steps: Authenticate and obtain a valid 3-part auth_token
Send any authenticated request to trigger middleware
Verify middleware re-sets auth_token cookie with maxAge: 86400 (1-day sliding window)
Verify the cookie value remains the same token (not re-signed), just the cookie maxAge is refreshed
test_cases.expected_result: Middleware re-sets the auth_token cookie with maxAge=86400 on every authenticated request, extending the sliding 1-day cookie expiry while the 7-day absolute token expiry (604800s) is enforced by verifyUserIdWithMeta

test_cases.title: Successful logout clears auth cookie
steps: Authenticate with valid credentials to receive auth_token cookie
POST /api/auth/logout
Verify the auth_token cookie is cleared (set to empty or expired)
Attempt to access a protected endpoint with the cleared cookie
test_cases.expected_result: Cookie is removed and subsequent requests are unauthenticated, returning 401

test_cases.title: Session persists across page navigations via getCurrentUser cache
steps: Authenticate and receive auth_token cookie

Call getCurrentUser which reads cookie via cookies().get('auth_token'), calls verifyUserIdWithMeta to parse 3-part token, then isTokenIssuedAtValid to check Redis user:minIssuedAt:{userId}, then fetchUserWithCache
Navigate to multiple protected pages within the same server request
Verify getCurrentUser returns the same user object due to React cache() deduplication
test_cases.expected_result: The auth_token cookie persists across navigations. getCurrentUser is wrapped with React cache() so within a single request it resolves the same user without repeated DB/Redis calls

test_cases.title: Valid 3-part token is accepted by verifyUserIdWithMeta
steps: Call signUserId(42) which creates token '42.{issuedAt}.{HMAC-SHA256 of 42.{issuedAt}}'
Verify the signature part is exactly 64 lowercase hex characters matching /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/
Pass the token to verifyUserIdWithMeta
Verify it returns { userId: 42, issuedAt: <unix timestamp> } with userId > 0 and issuedAt > 0
test_cases.expected_result: Token is parsed into 3 parts, signature verified via timingSafeEqual comparing Buffer.from(signature,'utf-8') with Buffer.from(expectedSignature,'utf-8'), and TokenMeta { userId: 42, issuedAt } is returned

test_cases.title: Legacy 2-part token accepted before cutoff date 2026-03-18
steps: Create a legacy token with format userId.signature (2 parts, no issuedAt) using HMAC-SHA256 of just the userId string
Ensure Date.now() returns a value <= new Date('2026-03-18T00:00:00Z').getTime() (1742256000000)
Pass token to verifyUserIdWithMeta
Verify parts.length === 2 branch is entered and Date.now() > LEGACY_TOKEN_CUTOFF is false
test_cases.expected_result: Legacy 2-part token is accepted, returns { userId, issuedAt: 0 } because legacy tokens have no issuedAt and default to epoch 0

test_cases.title: Legacy 2-part token rejected after cutoff date 2026-03-18
steps: Create a legacy token with format userId.signature (2 parts)
Ensure Date.now() returns a value > 1742256000000 (2026-03-18T00:00:00Z)
Pass token to verifyUserIdWithMeta
Verify the parts.length === 2 branch checks Date.now() > LEGACY_TOKEN_CUTOFF and returns null
test_cases.expected_result: Legacy 2-part token is rejected after the 2026-03-18 cutoff date because Date.now() > LEGACY_TOKEN_CUTOFF evaluates to true, returning null immediately

test_cases.title: Legacy 2-part token on exact cutoff boundary 2026-03-18T00:00:00Z
steps: Create a legacy token with format userId.signature
Mock Date.now() to return exactly 1742256000000 (new Date('2026-03-18T00:00:00Z').getTime())
Pass token to verifyUserIdWithMeta
Verify the check Date.now() > LEGACY_TOKEN_CUTOFF evaluates to false because 1742256000000 > 1742256000000 is false
test_cases.expected_result: At exactly the cutoff timestamp, the legacy token IS still accepted because the comparison uses strict greater-than (>), not >=. The token at the exact boundary passes validation and returns { userId, issuedAt: 0 }

test_cases.title: Malformed token with 4 or more dot-separated parts rejected
steps: Create a token string with 4 dot-separated parts: 'userId.issuedAt.signature.extra'
Pass to verifyUserIdWithMeta
Verify parts.length is 4, which matches neither the === 3 nor === 2 branches
Verify the function falls through to the final return null on line 87
test_cases.expected_result: Token with parts.length !== 2 and parts.length !== 3 is rejected; verifyUserIdWithMeta returns null because no branch handles 4+ parts

test_cases.title: Malformed token with empty parts rejected
steps: Create tokens with empty segments: '.issuedAt.signature', 'userId..signature', 'userId.issuedAt.'
Pass each to verifyUserIdWithMeta
Verify the check !userIdStr || !issuedAtStr || !signature catches the empty string (falsy) and returns null
test_cases.expected_result: All tokens with empty dot-separated segments are rejected because empty string is falsy, triggering the early return null at line 40

test_cases.title: Token with non-numeric userId rejected
steps: Create a token 'abc.1700000000.{valid64hexchars}'
Pass to verifyUserIdWithMeta
Verify signature format check passes (64 hex chars)
Verify timingSafeEqual would fail because HMAC of 'abc.1700000000' won't match the forged signature
Even if signature matched, parseInt('abc', 10) returns NaN, Number.isFinite(NaN) is false, triggering return null at line 54
test_cases.expected_result: Token rejected: either timingSafeEqual fails on signature mismatch (returning null at line 50), or if signature somehow matched, parseInt('abc',10) returns NaN which fails Number.isFinite check at line 54

test_cases.title: Token with issuedAt = 0 rejected by positive check
steps: Create a 3-part token with issuedAt set to 0
Generate valid HMAC-SHA256 signature for '{userId}.0'
Pass to verifyUserIdWithMeta
Verify issuedAt = parseInt('0', 10) = 0, then check issuedAt <= 0 triggers return null at line 55
test_cases.expected_result: Token with issuedAt = 0 is rejected because the check !Number.isFinite(issuedAt) || issuedAt <= 0 catches zero, returning null. Note: the code enforces issuedAt > 0 for 3-part tokens

test_cases.title: timingSafeEqual prevents timing-based signature guessing
steps: Create multiple tokens with incrementally correct signature bytes
Measure response time for each token verification via verifyUserIdWithMeta
Compare timing variance between a completely wrong signature and a partially correct one
Verify line 50 uses crypto.timingSafeEqual(sigBuf, expectedBuf) where both buffers are from Buffer.from(str, 'utf-8')
test_cases.expected_result: Response times are constant regardless of how many signature bytes match, preventing timing side-channel attacks. timingSafeEqual at line 50 compares in constant time

test_cases.title: timingSafeEqual handles different-length buffers via tokensMatch catch
steps: Call tokensMatch('short', 'muchlongerstring') from lib/security/tokens.ts
Verify crypto.timingSafeEqual throws RangeError because Buffer.from('short').length !== Buffer.from('muchlongerstring').length
Verify the catch block on line 8 returns false
test_cases.expected_result: tokensMatch returns false when inputs differ in byte length, catching the RangeError from timingSafeEqual without leaking length information

test_cases.title: tokensMatch returns false for null or empty inputs
steps: Call tokensMatch(null, 'valid') from lib/security/tokens.ts
Call tokensMatch('valid', null)
Call tokensMatch(null, null)
Call tokensMatch('', 'valid')
Call tokensMatch('valid', '')
Verify the guard !a || !b at line 4 short-circuits to return false
test_cases.expected_result: All calls return false immediately at line 4 because !null and !'' are both true, short-circuiting before reaching timingSafeEqual

test_cases.title: Rate limiting on login: 5 requests per IP per 15 minutes
steps: Send 5 POST /api/auth/login requests from the same IP within 15 minutes
Verify RATE_LIMITS.login config: { prefix: 'login', max: 5, windowSeconds: 900 }
Send a 6th login request from the same IP
Verify checkRateLimit increments Redis key rl:login:{ip} and count > 5 triggers 429
test_cases.expected_result: First 5 requests processed normally. 6th request returns 429 with body { error: 'Rate limit exceeded. Please try again later.' } and header Retry-After: 900

test_cases.title: Rate limiting on login per account: 10 requests per email per 30 minutes
steps: Send 10 POST /api/auth/login requests for the same email from different IPs within 30 minutes
Verify RATE_LIMITS.loginAccount config: { prefix: 'login-acct', max: 10, windowSeconds: 1800 }
Send an 11th login request for the same email from a new IP
Verify Redis key rl:login-acct:{email} count > 10 triggers 429
test_cases.expected_result: First 10 requests processed. 11th request returns 429 with body { error: 'Rate limit exceeded. Please try again later.' } and header Retry-After: 1800

test_cases.title: Rate limiting on registration: 3 requests per IP per 15 minutes
steps: Send 3 POST /api/auth/register requests from the same IP within 15 minutes
Verify RATE_LIMITS.register config: { prefix: 'register', max: 3, windowSeconds: 900 }
Send a 4th registration request from the same IP
Verify Redis key rl:register:{ip} count > 3 triggers 429
test_cases.expected_result: First 3 registrations processed. 4th request returns 429 with body { error: 'Rate limit exceeded. Please try again later.' } and header Retry-After: 900

test_cases.title: Rate limit resets after time window expires
steps: Send 5 POST /api/auth/login requests from the same IP
Verify the 6th request returns 429
Wait 900 seconds (15 minutes) for the Redis key rl:login:{ip} to expire via EXPIRE command
Send another login request from the same IP
test_cases.expected_result: After 900-second window expires, the Redis key is automatically deleted by EXPIRE, counter resets to 1, and login requests are accepted again

test_cases.title: revokeUserSessions sets minIssuedAt to current timestamp in Redis
steps: Authenticate user with userId=42
Call revokeUserSessions(42) from lib/session.ts
Verify redis.set is called with key 'user:minIssuedAt:42', value String(Math.floor(Date.now()/1000)), 'EX', 604800
Verify the Redis key TTL is 604800 seconds (SESSION_REVOKE_TTL matching TOKEN_MAX_AGE_SECONDS)
test_cases.expected_result: Redis key user:minIssuedAt:42 is set to the current Unix timestamp with a 7-day TTL, invalidating all tokens issued before this timestamp

test_cases.title: Tokens issued before session revocation are rejected
steps: Authenticate user 42 and receive token with issuedAt = T1
Call revokeUserSessions(42) which sets Redis key user:minIssuedAt:42 = T2 where T2 > T1
Call isTokenIssuedAtValid(42, T1) from lib/session.ts
Verify redis.get('user:minIssuedAt:42') returns T2
Verify the check T1 >= parseInt(T2, 10) evaluates to false
test_cases.expected_result: isTokenIssuedAtValid returns false because issuedAt (T1) < minIssuedAt (T2). getCurrentUser at line 119 checks this and returns null, rejecting the old token

test_cases.title: Tokens issued after session revocation are accepted
steps: Call revokeUserSessions(42) which sets Redis user:minIssuedAt:42 = T1
Authenticate user 42 again via signUserId(42), receiving token with issuedAt = T2 where T2 > T1
Call isTokenIssuedAtValid(42, T2)
Verify T2 >= parseInt(T1, 10) evaluates to true
test_cases.expected_result: isTokenIssuedAtValid returns true because new issuedAt (T2) >= minIssuedAt (T1). The comparison uses >= so tokens issued at the exact same second as revocation are accepted

test_cases.title: Token at exactly TOKEN_MAX_AGE_SECONDS (604800) boundary is accepted
steps: Create a 3-part token with issuedAt = (Math.floor(Date.now()/1000) - 604800)
Generate valid HMAC-SHA256 signature
Pass to verifyUserIdWithMeta
Verify the check at line 59: now - issuedAt > TOKEN_MAX_AGE_SECONDS evaluates to 604800 > 604800 which is false
test_cases.expected_result: Token at exactly 604800 seconds old IS accepted because the comparison uses strict greater-than (>), not >=. The value 604800 is NOT greater than 604800, so the token passes the expiration check

test_cases.title: Token one second past TOKEN_MAX_AGE_SECONDS is rejected
steps: Create a 3-part token with issuedAt = (Math.floor(Date.now()/1000) - 604801)
Generate valid HMAC-SHA256 signature
Pass to verifyUserIdWithMeta
Verify the check now - issuedAt > TOKEN_MAX_AGE_SECONDS evaluates to 604801 > 604800 which is true
test_cases.expected_result: Token that is 604801 seconds old (7 days + 1 second) is rejected because 604801 > 604800 is true, returning null at line 59

test_cases.title: Token one second before TOKEN_MAX_AGE_SECONDS is accepted
steps: Create a 3-part token with issuedAt = (Math.floor(Date.now()/1000) - 604799)
Generate valid HMAC-SHA256 signature
Pass to verifyUserIdWithMeta
Verify the check now - issuedAt > TOKEN_MAX_AGE_SECONDS evaluates to 604799 > 604800 which is false
test_cases.expected_result: Token that is 604799 seconds old (7 days - 1 second) is accepted because 604799 > 604800 is false

test_cases.title: Redis cache miss falls back to database for user fetch
steps: Authenticate with a valid token for userId=42
Clear Redis key user:session:42 so fetchUserWithCache gets a cache miss
Call getCurrentUser which triggers fetchUserWithCache(42)
Verify redis.get('user:session:42') returns null, falling through to prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id: 42 }, select: USER_SELECT })
Verify redis.set('user:session:42', JSON.stringify(user), 'EX', 300) is called to populate cache
test_cases.expected_result: When Redis cache misses, fetchUserWithCache falls through to DB query at line 48, then populates Redis cache with 300-second (5-minute) TTL at line 57

test_cases.title: Redis cache write failure does not block authentication
steps: Authenticate with a valid token for userId=42
Simulate redis.set throwing an error when populating cache at line 57
Call getCurrentUser which triggers fetchUserWithCache
Verify the catch block at line 58 silently swallows the error (comment: 'Non-critical')
Verify user data from DB is still returned to the caller
test_cases.expected_result: Authentication succeeds using database data even when Redis cache write fails, because the redis.set at line 57 is fire-and-forget with a catch that swallows errors

test_cases.title: Redis down during session revocation check fails open
steps: Authenticate with a valid token for userId=42
Simulate redis.get throwing an error in isTokenIssuedAtValid at line 34
Verify the catch block at line 37 logs a warning and returns true
Verify getCurrentUser proceeds to fetchUserWithCache
test_cases.expected_result: When Redis is down, isTokenIssuedAtValid catches the error at line 37, logs 'Redis unavailable for session revocation check, allowing token', and returns true (fail open), maintaining service availability

test_cases.title: auth_token cookie has httpOnly flag set in middleware
steps: Authenticate with valid credentials
Trigger middleware which calls response.cookies.set('auth_token', authToken, { httpOnly: true, ... }) at line 118
Inspect the Set-Cookie response header for auth_token
Verify the HttpOnly attribute is present
test_cases.expected_result: The auth_token cookie has HttpOnly flag set at middleware line 119, preventing client-side JavaScript document.cookie from reading the token

test_cases.title: auth_token cookie has secure flag in production
steps: Set process.env.NODE_ENV = 'production'
Authenticate with valid credentials
Verify middleware sets cookie with secure: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production' which evaluates to true
Inspect Set-Cookie header for Secure attribute
test_cases.expected_result: The auth_token cookie has Secure flag in production (middleware line 120: secure: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'), ensuring it is only sent over HTTPS

test_cases.title: auth_token cookie has sameSite=lax attribute
steps: Authenticate with valid credentials
Verify middleware sets cookie with sameSite: 'lax' at line 123
Inspect Set-Cookie header for SameSite=Lax attribute
test_cases.expected_result: The auth_token cookie has SameSite=Lax preventing it from being sent with cross-origin POST requests while allowing top-level GET navigations

test_cases.title: auth_token cookie path is set to /
steps: Authenticate with valid credentials
Verify middleware sets cookie with path: '/' at line 122
Inspect Set-Cookie header for Path=/ attribute
test_cases.expected_result: The auth_token cookie has Path=/ (middleware line 122), making it available across all routes

test_cases.title: No auth_token cookie set on failed authentication
steps: POST /api/auth/login with valid email but incorrect password
Verify bcrypt.compare returns false for the wrong password
Inspect the response headers for Set-Cookie
Verify no auth_token cookie is set
Verify response returns 401 with a generic 'invalid credentials' error message
test_cases.expected_result: Failed login does not set any auth_token cookie and returns 401. The error message is generic to prevent email enumeration

test_cases.title: Concurrent login sessions from different devices are both valid
steps: Authenticate user 42 from Device A via signUserId(42), receiving token with issuedAt = T1
Authenticate same user 42 from Device B via signUserId(42), receiving token with issuedAt = T2 where T2 > T1
Pass both tokens to verifyUserIdWithMeta concurrently
Verify both return valid TokenMeta since each has a valid signature and issuedAt
test_cases.expected_result: Both concurrent sessions remain valid because signUserId generates independent tokens with unique issuedAt timestamps, and there is no single-session enforcement

test_cases.title: User deleted after token issued returns null from getCurrentUser
steps: Authenticate user 42 and receive valid auth_token
Delete user 42 from the database via prisma.user.delete
Delete Redis key user:session:42
Call getCurrentUser with the old token
Verify verifyUserIdWithMeta returns valid TokenMeta (signature is still valid)
Verify fetchUserWithCache(42) calls prisma.user.findUnique which returns null
test_cases.expected_result: getCurrentUser returns null because fetchUserWithCache returns null from DB at line 53, even though the token signature is cryptographically valid

test_cases.title: bcrypt hash comparison for password verification
steps: Register user with password 'TestPass123!'
Verify the stored password is a bcrypt hash (starts with $2b$ or $2a$)
Attempt login with correct password 'TestPass123!'
Attempt login with incorrect password 'TestPass123'
Verify bcrypt.compare is used, not direct string comparison
test_cases.expected_result: Password is stored as bcrypt hash. Correct password passes bcrypt.compare, incorrect password fails bcrypt.compare. Direct comparison would never match because hash is always different

test_cases.title: Login with non-existent email returns generic error
steps: POST /api/auth/login with email 'nonexistent@example.com' that does not exist in the database
Verify the response does not distinguish between 'email not found' and 'wrong password'
Verify response returns a generic authentication error message
test_cases.expected_result: Login returns a generic 'invalid credentials' error without disclosing whether the email is registered, preventing email enumeration attacks

test_cases.title: Registration with duplicate email fails
steps: Register a user with email test@example.com
Attempt to register a second user with the same email test@example.com
Verify the database unique constraint on email prevents duplicate
Verify the error is returned without exposing database schema details
test_cases.expected_result: Second registration attempt fails with an error indicating the email is already registered. Prisma P2002 (unique constraint) may be wrapped by wrapPrismaError into Hebrew message

test_cases.title: Token with no dot separators rejected
steps: Set auth_token cookie to 'randomGarbageToken123' (no dots)
Pass to verifyUserIdWithMeta
Verify token.split('.') produces array with length 1
Verify parts.length === 3 is false and parts.length === 2 is false
Verify function returns null at the final return statement (line 87)
test_cases.expected_result: Token with parts.length === 1 (no dot separators) is rejected, verifyUserIdWithMeta returns null at line 87. Middleware also checks parts.length < 2 at line 103 and redirects to /login

test_cases.title: getCurrentUser with expired Redis cache refreshes from DB
steps: Authenticate and populate Redis key user:session:42 with 300-second TTL
Wait for the 300-second TTL to expire
Call getCurrentUser which triggers fetchUserWithCache(42)
Verify redis.get('user:session:42') returns null (expired)
Verify prisma.user.findUnique is called and cache is repopulated with new 300-second TTL
test_cases.expected_result: After Redis cache TTL expires (USER_CACHE_TTL = 300 seconds), fetchUserWithCache fetches fresh data from DB and repopulates Redis with new 300-second TTL

test_cases.title: Missing auth_token cookie on protected endpoint
steps: Send a request to a protected page (e.g., /dashboard) without any auth_token cookie
Verify middleware at line 93 checks request.cookies.get('auth_token')?.value and gets undefined
Verify middleware returns NextResponse.redirect(new URL('/login', request.url)) at line 97
test_cases.expected_result: Request without auth_token cookie is redirected to /login by middleware. getCurrentUser would also return null because cookieStore.get('auth_token') is undefined at line 107

test_cases.title: Token verification throughput under sustained load
steps: Generate 10,000 valid 3-part tokens via signUserId for different userIds
Call verifyUserIdWithMeta for each token sequentially and measure total time

Verify each call performs: split('.'), regex test /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/, createHmac('sha256',SECRET).update(data).digest('hex'), Buffer.from + timingSafeEqual, two parseInt, two Number.isFinite, one Date.now, one subtraction
test_cases.expected_result: Each verifyUserIdWithMeta call completes in under 1ms on average. The function is synchronous with no I/O, only CPU-bound HMAC-SHA256 computation. 10,000 verifications should complete in under 5 seconds total

test_cases.title: Parallel login attempts from same account do not corrupt rate limit counter
steps: Send 5 concurrent POST /api/auth/login requests for the same email from the same IP simultaneously
Verify Redis MULTI/EXEC atomic INCR+EXPIRE at checkRateLimit ensures each request increments rl:login:{ip} atomically
Verify the 5th request gets count=5 which is not > 5 so it passes
Send a 6th concurrent request and verify it gets count=6 > 5 and receives 429
test_cases.expected_result: Concurrent requests do not race past the rate limit because Redis MULTI/EXEC with INCR is atomic. Each request gets a unique incremented count. No request can bypass the limit of 5

test_cases.title: Full authentication flow: login -> getCurrentUser -> middleware refresh -> logout
steps: POST /api/auth/login with valid credentials, receive auth_token cookie with 3-part token
Send GET /dashboard which triggers middleware: validate token format (parts 2-3, signature 64 hex), re-set cookie with maxAge=86400

Server-side getCurrentUser: reads cookie -> verifyUserIdWithMeta parses token -> isTokenIssuedAtValid checks Redis user:minIssuedAt:{userId} -> fetchUserWithCache checks Redis user:session:{userId} then DB
POST /api/auth/logout to clear the cookie
Verify subsequent requests redirect to /login
test_cases.expected_result: Complete auth lifecycle works end-to-end: login sets cookie, middleware validates and refreshes cookie on each request, getCurrentUser resolves user through the full verification chain, logout clears cookie and subsequent requests redirect to /login

test_cases.title: signUserId produces deterministic signature for same userId and issuedAt
steps: Mock Date.now to return a fixed value (e.g., 1700000000000) so issuedAt = 1700000000
Call signUserId(42) twice with the same mocked time
Verify both calls produce identical tokens: '42.1700000000.{same_signature}'
Verify the signature equals createHmac('sha256', SECRET).update('42.1700000000').digest('hex')
test_cases.expected_result: HMAC-SHA256 is deterministic: same input (userId.issuedAt) with same SECRET always produces the same 64-character hex signature. Token format is exactly '{userId}.{issuedAt}.{signature}'

test_cases.title: Unauthenticated user accessing protected page is redirected to /login
steps: Send GET request to /dashboard without auth_token cookie
Verify middleware at line 93-98: authToken is undefined, middleware returns NextResponse.redirect(new URL('/login', request.url))
Verify the redirect is a 307 temporary redirect (Next.js default)
Verify the user sees the login page
test_cases.expected_result: User without auth_token is seamlessly redirected to /login by middleware. No error page is shown. The redirect preserves the request URL for potential post-login redirect

test_cases.title: Middleware token format validation matches verifyUserIdWithMeta acceptance
steps: Create a valid 3-part token via signUserId(42)
Verify middleware format check at lines 102-109: parts.length >= 2 && parts.length <= 3, signaturePart.length === 64, /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/ matches
Verify the same token passes verifyUserIdWithMeta: parts.length === 3, signature regex check at line 41
Create a token with uppercase hex signature 'A' in position: verify middleware regex /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/ rejects it
Verify verifyUserIdWithMeta also rejects uppercase hex at line 41: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/
test_cases.expected_result: Middleware and verifyUserIdWithMeta use the same validation rules for token format: 2-3 parts, final part must be exactly 64 lowercase hex characters. No token passes middleware but fails verifyUserIdWithMeta or vice versa for format checks

test_cases.title: Middleware validates signature is exactly 64 hex chars, rejects 63 or 65
steps: Create token with 63-character hex signature part
Send request through middleware
Verify middleware check signaturePart.length !== 64 at line 107 fails, redirecting to /login
Create token with 65-character hex signature part
Verify same rejection at line 107
test_cases.expected_result: Middleware enforces exactly 64 hex characters for the signature part. Both 63 and 65 character signatures are rejected with redirect to /login, matching the HMAC-SHA256 digest('hex') output length

test_cases.title: SESSION_SECRET missing causes immediate startup crash
steps: Unset or remove the SESSION_SECRET environment variable
Attempt to import lib/auth.ts
Verify the top-level guard at lines 5-9 throws Error('FATAL: SESSION_SECRET environment variable is not set. Refusing to start without a session secret.')
test_cases.expected_result: Application refuses to start when SESSION_SECRET is missing, throwing a FATAL error at module load time. This prevents running with no signing secret which would make all tokens forgeable

test_cases.title: Admin user has all permission flags implicitly
steps: Create user with role 'admin' and empty permissions object
Call hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewDashboard')
Call hasUserFlag(user, 'canManageTables')
Call hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewFiles')
Verify all calls return true due to the early return at line 57: if (user.role === 'admin') return true
test_cases.expected_result: hasUserFlag returns true for every flag when user.role is 'admin', bypassing the permissions object entirely via the admin check at line 57

test_cases.title: Manager user has elevated read access to all tables
steps: Create user with role 'manager'
Call canReadTable(user, 1) for table ID 1
Call canReadTable(user, 999) for arbitrary table ID
Verify both return true due to line 68: if (user.role === 'admin' || user.role === 'manager') return true
test_cases.expected_result: canReadTable returns true for all table IDs when user.role is 'manager' via the early return at line 68

test_cases.title: Basic user with specific permission flags granted
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and permissions: { canViewDashboard: true, canViewTables: true, canViewFiles: true }
Call hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewDashboard') and verify true via !!user.permissions['canViewDashboard'] === !!true === true
Call hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewTables') and verify true
Call hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewFiles') and verify true
Call hasUserFlag(user, 'canManageTables') and verify false via !!user.permissions['canManageTables'] === !!undefined === false
test_cases.expected_result: Basic user has exactly the flags set in their permissions object. hasUserFlag at line 58 uses !!user.permissions?.[flag] which converts truthy values to true and undefined/false to false

test_cases.title: Each USER_FLAGS key is correctly enforced for basic user
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and a single permission flag set: { canViewAutomations: true }
Iterate through all 31 USER_FLAGS keys from lib/permissions.ts
Call hasUserFlag for each key
Verify only canViewAutomations returns true, all other 30 flags return false
test_cases.expected_result: Only the specifically granted flag returns true; all other flags return false because user.permissions['otherFlag'] is undefined, and !!undefined === false

test_cases.title: Admin bypass works for hasUserFlag across all 31 flags
steps: Create user with role 'admin' and permissions: undefined
Call hasUserFlag for every key in USER_FLAGS array (31 keys)
Verify every single call returns true
test_cases.expected_result: Admin role returns true for all 31 flags via the early return at line 57 (if user.role === 'admin' return true), even with undefined permissions. The permissions object is never checked

test_cases.title: Basic user denied access without required flag
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and permissions: { canViewDashboard: true }
Call hasUserFlag(user, 'canManageWorkers')
Call hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewFinance')
Verify both return false because user.permissions['canManageWorkers'] is undefined
test_cases.expected_result: Basic user is denied access to flags not present in their permissions object. !!undefined === false at line 58

test_cases.title: Admin can read all tables via canReadTable
steps: Create user with role 'admin' and no tablePermissions
Call canReadTable(user, 1), canReadTable(user, 999), canReadTable(user, 0)
Verify all return true due to line 68: user.role === 'admin' check
test_cases.expected_result: canReadTable returns true for all table IDs when user.role is 'admin', regardless of tablePermissions

test_cases.title: Basic user with read permission can read table
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and tablePermissions: { '5': 'read' }
Call canReadTable(user, 5)
Verify user.tablePermissions?.['5'] returns 'read'
Verify perm === 'read' || perm === 'write' evaluates to true
test_cases.expected_result: canReadTable returns true for basic user with tablePermissions['5'] === 'read' at line 71

test_cases.title: Basic user with write permission can also read table (write implies read)
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and tablePermissions: { '5': 'write' }
Call canReadTable(user, 5)
Verify user.tablePermissions?.['5'] returns 'write'
Verify perm === 'read' || perm === 'write' evaluates to true because 'write' matches
test_cases.expected_result: canReadTable returns true because write access implies read access at line 71: perm === 'write' is true

test_cases.title: Basic user with no table permission is denied read access
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and tablePermissions: { '5': 'read' }
Call canReadTable(user, 10) for a table ID not in tablePermissions
Verify user.tablePermissions?.['10'] returns undefined
Verify undefined === 'read' || undefined === 'write' evaluates to false
test_cases.expected_result: canReadTable returns false for table ID 10 because it is not a key in tablePermissions

test_cases.title: Basic user with 'none' table permission is denied access
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and tablePermissions: { '5': 'none' }
Call canReadTable(user, 5)
Verify user.tablePermissions?.['5'] returns 'none'
Verify 'none' === 'read' || 'none' === 'write' evaluates to false
test_cases.expected_result: canReadTable returns false because 'none' does not match 'read' or 'write' at line 71

test_cases.title: Admin can write to all tables via canWriteTable
steps: Create user with role 'admin' and no allowedWriteTableIds
Call canWriteTable(user, 1), canWriteTable(user, 999)
Verify all return true due to line 81-83: if (user.role === 'admin') return true
test_cases.expected_result: canWriteTable returns true for all table IDs when user.role is 'admin', skipping allowedWriteTableIds and tablePermissions checks entirely

test_cases.title: Manager can write to tables in allowedWriteTableIds
steps: Create user with role 'manager' and allowedWriteTableIds: [1, 5, 10]
Call canWriteTable(user, 5)
Verify line 85: user.allowedWriteTableIds.includes(5) returns true
test_cases.expected_result: canWriteTable returns true for manager when tableId is in allowedWriteTableIds array via Array.includes at line 85

test_cases.title: Manager cannot write to tables not in allowedWriteTableIds
steps: Create user with role 'manager' and allowedWriteTableIds: [1, 5, 10]
Call canWriteTable(user, 7)
Verify line 85: user.allowedWriteTableIds.includes(7) returns false
test_cases.expected_result: canWriteTable returns false for manager when tableId 7 is not in allowedWriteTableIds [1, 5, 10]

test_cases.title: Basic user with write table permission can write
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and tablePermissions: { '5': 'write' }
Call canWriteTable(user, 5)
Verify line 88-89: user.tablePermissions?.['5'] returns 'write', perm === 'write' is true
test_cases.expected_result: canWriteTable returns true for basic user whose tablePermissions has 'write' for table 5 at line 89

test_cases.title: Basic user with read-only table permission cannot write
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and tablePermissions: { '5': 'read' }
Call canWriteTable(user, 5)
Verify line 88-89: user.tablePermissions?.['5'] returns 'read', perm === 'write' is false
test_cases.expected_result: canWriteTable returns false because 'read' !== 'write' at line 89. Read permission does NOT imply write access

test_cases.title: User cannot escalate their own role
steps: Authenticate as a user with role 'basic'
Attempt to call the user update API (PATCH /api/users/{ownId}) to change own role to 'admin'
Verify canManageUsers(user) returns false at line 97 because user.role !== 'admin'
Verify the API rejects the role change with 403
test_cases.expected_result: Self role escalation is denied. canManageUsers checks user.role === 'admin' (line 97), so basic users cannot modify any user including themselves

test_cases.title: Admin can change other user roles
steps: Authenticate as an admin user
Call canManageUsers(adminUser) and verify it returns true (line 97: user.role === 'admin')
PATCH /api/users/{targetId} to update a basic user's role to 'manager'
Verify the role change is persisted in the database
Verify invalidateUserCache(targetId) is called to clear Redis key user:session:{targetId}
test_cases.expected_result: Admin can successfully change another user's role. After update, invalidateUserCache is called to ensure the cached permissions are cleared immediately

test_cases.title: Manager cannot manage users
steps: Authenticate as a manager user
Call canManageUsers(managerUser) and verify it returns false (line 97: 'manager' !== 'admin')
Attempt to PATCH /api/users/{targetId} to change a basic user's role
Verify the API rejects the request with 403
test_cases.expected_result: Manager is denied user management since canManageUsers returns false for role 'manager'. Only role === 'admin' returns true

test_cases.title: User permissions are cached in Redis with 5-minute TTL
steps: Authenticate and trigger fetchUserWithCache for userId=42
Verify redis.get('user:session:42') is called first (cache check)
On cache miss, verify prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id: 42 }, select: USER_SELECT }) is called
Verify redis.set('user:session:42', JSON.stringify(user), 'EX', 300) is called with USER_CACHE_TTL=300
test_cases.expected_result: First call populates Redis cache with key user:session:42 and 300-second (5-minute) TTL. Subsequent calls within TTL return cached JSON without DB queries

test_cases.title: Cache invalidated on permission change via invalidateUserCache
steps: Authenticate user 42 and populate Redis cache key user:session:42
Update user's permissions via admin API (PATCH /api/users/42)
Verify invalidateUserCache(42) is called, which executes redis.del('user:session:42')
Call fetchUserWithCache(42) again
Verify Redis cache miss triggers fresh DB query
test_cases.expected_result: After permission change, invalidateUserCache deletes Redis key user:session:42, forcing the next fetchUserWithCache call to read from DB with updated permissions

test_cases.title: Stale cache serves old permissions until TTL expires
steps: Authenticate user with permissions { canViewDashboard: true } and populate Redis cache
Directly update user's permissions in database to { canViewDashboard: false } WITHOUT calling invalidateUserCache
Call fetchUserWithCache before the 300-second TTL expires
Verify redis.get returns the stale cached data with canViewDashboard: true
test_cases.expected_result: Without explicit cache invalidation via invalidateUserCache, stale permissions are served from Redis for up to 300 seconds (USER_CACHE_TTL). This is a known trade-off documented in the invalidateUserCache JSDoc

test_cases.title: User cannot access another company's data due to companyId scoping
steps: Create userA with companyId=1 and userB with companyId=2
Authenticate as userA
Attempt to query data belonging to companyId=2 via any API endpoint
Verify all queries include WHERE companyId = user.companyId (from getCurrentUser)
test_cases.expected_result: Multi-tenancy is enforced. All data queries are scoped by the authenticated user's companyId, preventing cross-tenant access

test_cases.title: companyId is always enforced in database queries
steps: Authenticate as user with companyId=1
Issue multiple API calls: list tables, list records, list users
Inspect the generated Prisma queries
Verify every query includes WHERE companyId = 1
test_cases.expected_result: All database queries include companyId in the WHERE clause, preventing cross-tenant data leakage

test_cases.title: User with undefined permissions defaults to no access
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and permissions set to undefined
Call hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewDashboard')
Verify user.permissions is undefined, so user.permissions?.['canViewDashboard'] returns undefined via optional chaining
Verify !!undefined === false
test_cases.expected_result: hasUserFlag returns false when permissions is undefined. The ?. operator at line 58 short-circuits to undefined, then !! converts to false

test_cases.title: User with empty permissions object has no flags
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and permissions: {}
Call hasUserFlag for every key in USER_FLAGS (31 keys)
Verify all return false because {}['anyKey'] is undefined, and !!undefined is false
test_cases.expected_result: hasUserFlag returns false for all 31 flags when the permissions object exists but has no keys set

test_cases.title: User with null role defaults to no elevated access
steps: Create user object with role set to null
Call hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewDashboard') with permissions: { canViewDashboard: true }
Verify null === 'admin' is false at line 57, so admin bypass is skipped
Verify the flag check at line 58 still returns true because !!user.permissions['canViewDashboard'] === !!true === true
test_cases.expected_result: Null role does not match 'admin' string comparison, so the admin bypass is skipped. Explicit permissions still work correctly via line 58

test_cases.title: Basic user with undefined tablePermissions denied table access
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and tablePermissions: undefined
Call canReadTable(user, 5)
Verify user.tablePermissions?.['5'] returns undefined via optional chaining at line 70
Verify undefined === 'read' || undefined === 'write' is false
Call canWriteTable(user, 5)
Verify user.tablePermissions?.['5'] returns undefined via optional chaining at line 88
Verify undefined === 'write' is false
test_cases.expected_result: canReadTable and canWriteTable both return false when tablePermissions is undefined, via optional chaining at lines 70 and 88

test_cases.title: Manager with empty allowedWriteTableIds cannot write any table
steps: Create user with role 'manager' and allowedWriteTableIds: []
Call canWriteTable(user, 1)
Verify [].includes(1) returns false at line 85
Call canReadTable(user, 1)
Verify canReadTable returns true at line 68 because role === 'manager'
test_cases.expected_result: Manager with empty allowedWriteTableIds is denied write access to all tables (line 85: [].includes(id) is always false), but read access is still granted via canReadTable line 68

test_cases.title: Permission flag explicitly set to false is correctly denied
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and permissions: { canViewDashboard: false, canViewTables: true }
Call hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewDashboard')
Verify !!user.permissions['canViewDashboard'] === !!false === false
Call hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewTables')
Verify !!user.permissions['canViewTables'] === !!true === true
test_cases.expected_result: A flag explicitly set to false is correctly denied by the !!false conversion at line 58. This prevents accidentally treating an explicit false as different from undefined

test_cases.title: Basic user with empty allowedWriteTableIds array and no tablePermissions
steps: Create user with role 'basic', allowedWriteTableIds: [], tablePermissions: undefined
Call canWriteTable(user, 1)
Verify role is 'basic' so admin check at line 81 fails and manager check at line 84 fails
Verify user.tablePermissions?.['1'] returns undefined at line 88
Verify undefined === 'write' is false at line 89
test_cases.expected_result: Basic user with empty allowedWriteTableIds and undefined tablePermissions is denied all write access. The allowedWriteTableIds array is not checked for basic users (only for managers at line 85)

test_cases.title: Concurrent role changes do not produce inconsistent state
steps: Authenticate as admin user
Send two concurrent PATCH /api/users/42 requests: one changing role to 'manager', another changing role to 'admin'
Verify both requests call invalidateUserCache(42) after their respective database updates
Verify the final state in the database is consistent (last write wins)
Verify the Redis cache is cleared by both calls to invalidateUserCache
test_cases.expected_result: Concurrent role changes are handled by database-level serialization (Prisma update). Both invalidateUserCache calls clear Redis key user:session:42. The final DB state reflects the last successful write. No stale cache persists because both paths call invalidateUserCache

test_cases.title: User management rate limit: 10 operations per user per minute
steps: Authenticate as admin user with userId=1
Send 10 user management mutation requests within 60 seconds
Verify RATE_LIMITS.userManagement config: { prefix: 'user-mgmt', max: 10, windowSeconds: 60 }
Send an 11th request
Verify Redis key rl:user-mgmt:{userId} count > 10 triggers 429
test_cases.expected_result: First 10 user management requests succeed. 11th request returns 429 with body { error: 'Rate limit exceeded. Please try again later.' } and Retry-After: 60. This prevents bulk user manipulation attacks

test_cases.title: Permission change flows through cache invalidation to next request
steps: Authenticate as user 42 with role 'basic' and permissions: { canViewDashboard: true }
Verify fetchUserWithCache populates Redis key user:session:42 with canViewDashboard: true
Admin changes user 42's permissions to { canViewDashboard: false } via PATCH /api/users/42
Verify invalidateUserCache(42) deletes Redis key user:session:42
User 42 sends next request, getCurrentUser triggers fetchUserWithCache(42)
Verify Redis cache miss, DB query returns updated permissions with canViewDashboard: false
Verify hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewDashboard') now returns false
test_cases.expected_result: Full integration: permission change -> cache invalidation -> fresh DB fetch -> updated permission check. The user sees updated permissions on their very next request after admin change

test_cases.title: tablePermissions keys are string-typed table IDs matching canReadTable/canWriteTable lookups
steps: Create user with tablePermissions: { '5': 'write', '10': 'read' } (keys are strings)
Call canReadTable(user, 5) which does user.tablePermissions?.[(5).toString()] at line 70
Verify '5'.toString() === '5' matches the key
Call canReadTable(user, 5) with numeric tableId parameter
Verify the .toString() conversion at line 70 ensures numeric tableId matches string key
test_cases.expected_result: tablePermissions uses string keys in the JSON object, and canReadTable/canWriteTable convert numeric tableId to string via tableId.toString() at lines 70 and 88. This ensures consistent lookup regardless of whether tableId is passed as number

test_cases.title: Basic user without canViewDashboard flag sees appropriate access denied state
steps: Create user with role 'basic' and permissions: {} (no flags)
Navigate to /dashboard
Verify getCurrentUser returns the user object (authenticated)
Verify hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewDashboard') returns false
Verify the UI shows an appropriate access denied message rather than a raw error
test_cases.expected_result: Authenticated basic user without the canViewDashboard flag is shown a user-friendly access denied state, not a 500 error or blank page. The permission check hasUserFlag returns false cleanly

test_cases.title: canManageUsers only returns true for admin role, not manager or basic
steps: Create three users: admin (role='admin'), manager (role='manager'), basic (role='basic')
Call canManageUsers for each: canManageUsers({ role: 'admin' }) returns true
canManageUsers({ role: 'manager' }) returns false
canManageUsers({ role: 'basic' }) returns false
Verify line 97: return user.role === 'admin' uses strict equality
test_cases.expected_result: canManageUsers at line 97 uses strict equality (===) to check for 'admin'. Only role === 'admin' returns true. This prevents regression where manager might accidentally gain user management access

test_cases.title: canManageTables delegates to hasUserFlag for canManageTables flag
steps: Create admin user and call canManageTables(adminUser)
Verify canManageTables at line 104 calls hasUserFlag(user, 'canManageTables')
Verify hasUserFlag returns true for admin at line 57
Create basic user with permissions: { canManageTables: true }
Call canManageTables(basicUser) and verify it returns true
Create basic user with permissions: {} and verify canManageTables returns false
test_cases.expected_result: canManageTables at line 104-106 delegates entirely to hasUserFlag. Admin gets implicit access, basic users need explicit canManageTables: true in permissions

test_cases.title: USER_SELECT in fetchUserWithCache includes all permission-related fields
steps: Inspect USER_SELECT constant in lib/permissions-server.ts
Verify it includes: id, companyId, name, email, role, isPremium, allowedWriteTableIds, permissions, tablePermissions, company.name, company.slug
Verify fetchUserWithCache uses this select to query prisma.user.findUnique
Verify the returned user object has all fields needed by hasUserFlag, canReadTable, canWriteTable, canManageUsers
test_cases.expected_result: USER_SELECT includes all fields required for permission checks: role (for admin bypass), permissions (for hasUserFlag), tablePermissions (for canReadTable/canWriteTable), allowedWriteTableIds (for manager write check), companyId (for multi-tenancy)

test_cases.title: Basic user cannot write to table with only read permission in tablePermissions
steps: Create user with role 'basic', tablePermissions: { '5': 'read', '10': 'write' }
Call canWriteTable(user, 5) - verify returns false because 'read' !== 'write' at line 89
Call canWriteTable(user, 10) - verify returns true because 'write' === 'write' at line 89
Call canReadTable(user, 5) - verify returns true because 'read' === 'read' at line 71
Call canReadTable(user, 10) - verify returns true because 'write' matches perm === 'write' at line 71
test_cases.expected_result: Read permission allows read but not write. Write permission allows both read and write. This asymmetry is by design: canReadTable accepts 'read' or 'write', canWriteTable only accepts 'write'

test_cases.title: Create ticket with valid fields and auto-calculated SLA from SlaPolicy
steps: Authenticate as user with canViewServiceCalls permission flag in same companyId
Ensure SlaPolicy exists for priority MEDIUM in the company (e.g., responseTimeMinutes=60, resolveTimeMinutes=480)
Call createTicket({ title: 'Network issue', description: 'VPN down', status: 'OPEN', priority: 'MEDIUM', type: 'SERVICE', clientId: <valid_same_company>, tags: ['network'] })
Verify returned ticket has status='OPEN', priority='MEDIUM', type='SERVICE', creatorId=currentUser.id, companyId=currentUser.companyId
Verify slaResponseDueDate is approximately now + 60 minutes and slaDueDate is approximately now + 480 minutes
Verify Inngest event 'ticket/notification' is NOT sent (no assignee different from creator)
test_cases.expected_result: Ticket is created with status OPEN, SLA response deadline = now + responseTimeMinutes, SLA resolve deadline = now + resolveTimeMinutes from MEDIUM SlaPolicy. Tags array contains ['network']. Activity log Inngest event is fired.

test_cases.title: Update ticket title and description with row-level locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE)
steps: Authenticate as user with canViewServiceCalls permission
Create a ticket with title='Original' in OPEN status
Call updateTicket(ticketId, { title: 'Updated Title', description: 'New desc' })
Verify the update uses an interactive transaction with SELECT FOR UPDATE on the Ticket row
Verify returned ticket has title='Updated Title', description='New desc'
Verify Inngest event 'ticket/activity-log' is sent with previousData containing old title and newData containing new title
test_cases.expected_result: Ticket title and description are updated atomically within a transaction using row-level locking. Inngest 'ticket/activity-log' event is fired with the diff. serviceMutation rate limit (30/user/min) is checked.

test_cases.title: Add comment to ticket via addTicketComment
steps: Authenticate as user with canViewServiceCalls permission
Create a ticket in companyId=user.companyId
Call addTicketComment(ticketId, 'This is a test comment', false)
Verify returned comment has content='This is a test comment', userId=currentUser.id, isInternal=false
Verify Inngest event 'ticket/notification' with type='comment' is sent if ticket has an assignee
test_cases.expected_result: Comment is created with content, userId, isInternal=false, and linked to the ticket. Inngest notification event is fired for assignee. serviceMutation rate limit (30/user/min prefix 'svc-mut') is checked.

test_cases.title: Transition ticket status OPEN to IN_PROGRESS fires status-change automation
steps: Create a ticket in OPEN status
Call updateTicket(ticketId, { status: 'IN_PROGRESS' })
Verify returned ticket has status='IN_PROGRESS'
Verify Inngest events array includes 'ticket/activity-log' AND 'ticket/status-change' with fromStatus='OPEN', toStatus='IN_PROGRESS'
Verify the fallback path queries AutomationRule with triggerType='TICKET_STATUS_CHANGE' and checks triggerConfig.fromStatus/toStatus matching
test_cases.expected_result: Ticket status changes from OPEN to IN_PROGRESS. Two Inngest events are fired: activity-log and status-change. If Inngest fails, direct fallback queries automation rules and sends notifications.

test_cases.title: Assign ticket to user in same company with cross-company validation
steps: Create a ticket in OPEN status in companyId=1
Call updateTicket(ticketId, { assigneeId: <userId_in_company_1> })
Verify validateUserInCompany(assigneeId, companyId) returns true
Verify returned ticket has assigneeId set
Verify Inngest event 'ticket/notification' with type='assignee', isNew=false is sent if assigneeId != currentUser.id
test_cases.expected_result: Ticket assigneeId is updated. Cross-company validation via validateUserInCompany passes. Inngest notification event is fired for the new assignee if different from current user.

test_cases.title: Close ticket from RESOLVED status
steps: Create a ticket and progress it through OPEN -> IN_PROGRESS -> RESOLVED via successive updateTicket calls
Call updateTicket(ticketId, { status: 'CLOSED' })
Verify returned ticket has status='CLOSED'
Call getClosedTickets() and verify the ticket appears in the returned items list
Verify the ticket no longer appears in getTickets() which filters status IN ['OPEN','IN_PROGRESS','WAITING','RESOLVED']
test_cases.expected_result: Ticket status is CLOSED. It appears in getClosedTickets (where status='CLOSED') and no longer in getTickets (which excludes CLOSED). Activity log records each transition.

test_cases.title: Restore closed ticket to OPEN via restoreTicket with SLA recalculation
steps: Have a ticket in CLOSED status with SlaPolicy configured for its priority
Call restoreTicket(ticketId, 'OPEN')
Verify the function validates 'OPEN' is in validRestoreStatuses Set(['OPEN','IN_PROGRESS','WAITING','RESOLVED'])
Verify transaction acquires row-level lock via SELECT FOR UPDATE
Verify ticket status is updated to OPEN
Verify slaDueDate is recalculated from now + slaPolicy.resolveTimeMinutes
Verify slaResponseDueDate is recalculated from now + slaPolicy.responseTimeMinutes (because newStatus='OPEN')
Verify TicketActivityLog is created with fieldName='status', oldValue='CLOSED', newValue='OPEN', oldLabel='סגור', newLabel='פתוח'
test_cases.expected_result: Ticket is restored to OPEN with row-level locking. SLA deadlines are recalculated from restoration timestamp using the ticket's priority SlaPolicy. Activity log entry is created directly in the transaction (not via Inngest).

test_cases.title: Full ticket status lifecycle OPEN -> IN_PROGRESS -> WAITING -> RESOLVED -> CLOSED
steps: Create ticket in OPEN status
updateTicket(id, { status: 'IN_PROGRESS' }) - verify success
updateTicket(id, { status: 'WAITING' }) - verify success
updateTicket(id, { status: 'RESOLVED' }) - verify success
updateTicket(id, { status: 'CLOSED' }) - verify success
Verify 4 Inngest 'ticket/status-change' events were fired with correct fromStatus/toStatus pairs
Verify 4 Inngest 'ticket/activity-log' events were fired
test_cases.expected_result: Each transition succeeds. All 5 TicketStatus enum values (OPEN, IN_PROGRESS, WAITING, RESOLVED, CLOSED) are traversed. Activity logs record every status change.

test_cases.title: Edit own comment on ticket via updateTicketComment
steps: Authenticate as userA with canViewServiceCalls
Add a comment to a ticket: addTicketComment(ticketId, 'original text')
Call updateTicketComment(commentId, 'updated text')
Verify the function checks comment.userId === user.id (ownership validation passes)
Verify prisma.ticketComment.updateMany is called with where: { id: commentId, ticket: { companyId } }
test_cases.expected_result: Comment content is updated to 'updated text'. Ownership check passes because userA.id === comment.userId. Company scoping prevents TOCTOU via ticket.companyId join.

test_cases.title: Delete own comment on ticket via deleteTicketComment
steps: Authenticate as userA with canViewServiceCalls
Add a comment to a ticket: addTicketComment(ticketId, 'to be deleted')
Call deleteTicketComment(commentId)
Verify the function checks comment.userId === user.id (ownership validation passes)
Verify prisma.ticketComment.deleteMany is called with where: { id: commentId, ticket: { companyId } }
Verify the comment no longer exists when querying ticket details
test_cases.expected_result: Comment is deleted. Ownership check passes because userA.id === comment.userId. Company scoping via ticket.companyId join prevents TOCTOU.

test_cases.title: SLA auto-calculation for CRITICAL priority ticket
steps: Configure SlaPolicy for CRITICAL priority: updateSlaPolicy({ priority: 'CRITICAL', responseTimeMinutes: 15, resolveTimeMinutes: 60 })
Create a ticket with priority 'CRITICAL': createTicket({ title: 'Server down', status: 'OPEN', priority: 'CRITICAL', type: 'SERVICE' })
Verify the createTicket function queries prisma.slaPolicy.findUnique with companyId_priority compound key
Verify returned ticket has slaResponseDueDate ~ now + 15 minutes and slaDueDate ~ now + 60 minutes
test_cases.expected_result: SLA deadlines are computed as Date.now() + responseTimeMinutes * 60 * 1000 and Date.now() + resolveTimeMinutes * 60 * 1000 based on CRITICAL SlaPolicy. Stored on the ticket record.

test_cases.title: SLA auto-calculation for LOW priority ticket
steps: Configure SlaPolicy for LOW priority: updateSlaPolicy({ priority: 'LOW', responseTimeMinutes: 480, resolveTimeMinutes: 2880 })
Create a ticket with priority 'LOW'
Verify slaResponseDueDate is approximately now + 480 minutes (8 hours)
Verify slaDueDate is approximately now + 2880 minutes (48 hours)
test_cases.expected_result: SLA deadlines for LOW priority are significantly longer than CRITICAL/HIGH. slaResponseDueDate = now + 480min, slaDueDate = now + 2880min.

test_cases.title: Permanently delete closed ticket with cascade via permanentlyDeleteTicket
steps: Create a ticket with comments (TicketComment), tags, and ensure activity logs (TicketActivityLog) and SLA breaches (SlaBreach) exist
Close the ticket
Call permanentlyDeleteTicket(ticketId)
Verify prisma.ticket.deleteMany is called with where: { id, companyId }
Verify cascade: TicketComment (onDelete: Cascade), TicketActivityLog (onDelete: Cascade), SlaBreach (onDelete: Cascade) are all removed
Verify the function invalidates Redis cache via redis.del('cache:metric:service:stats:{companyId}')
test_cases.expected_result: Ticket and all related data (comments, activity logs, SLA breaches) are cascade-deleted via Prisma onDelete: Cascade. Redis service stats cache is invalidated. revalidatePath('/service') and revalidatePath('/service/archive') are called.

test_cases.title: Add tags to ticket within MAX_TAGS=20 and MAX_TAG_LENGTH=100 limits
steps: Create a ticket
Call updateTicket(ticketId, { tags: ['bug', 'network', 'urgent', 'vpn', 'office'] })
Verify all 5 tags are stored in the ticket.tags String[] field
Verify createTicket validation: tags.length <= 20 and tags.every(t => t.length <= 100)
test_cases.expected_result: Tags array is persisted with 5 entries. Each tag is under 100 characters. MAX_TAGS=20 and MAX_TAG_LENGTH=100 constraints are satisfied.

test_cases.title: Kanban board drag-and-drop invokes updateTicket for status change
steps: Load the Kanban board showing tickets grouped by TicketStatus columns (OPEN, IN_PROGRESS, WAITING, RESOLVED)
Drag a ticket from OPEN column to IN_PROGRESS column (UI triggers updateTicket(id, { status: 'IN_PROGRESS' }))
Verify updateTicket is called with the same row-level locking transaction
Verify the Inngest 'ticket/status-change' event is fired
test_cases.expected_result: Ticket status is updated via the same updateTicket server action with row-level locking. Activity log and status-change automation events are fired identically to a form-based status change.

test_cases.title: Create ticket with title at exactly MAX_TITLE=500 characters
steps: Generate a string of exactly 500 characters
Call createTicket({ title: <500char_string>, status: 'OPEN', priority: 'MEDIUM', type: 'SERVICE' })
Verify createTicket validation: data.title.length > MAX_TITLE (500) returns false, so no error
Verify the ticket is created and title is stored in full
test_cases.expected_result: Ticket is created successfully. The validation check (!data.title || data.title.length > MAX_TITLE) passes because 500 is not > 500.

test_cases.title: Create ticket with description at exactly MAX_DESCRIPTION=10000 characters
steps: Generate a string of exactly 10000 characters
Call createTicket({ title: 'Test', description: <10000char_string>, status: 'OPEN', priority: 'MEDIUM', type: 'SERVICE' })
Verify validation: data.description.length > MAX_DESCRIPTION (10000) returns false
Verify the ticket is created with the full description
test_cases.expected_result: Ticket is created with the full 10000-character description. Validation passes because 10000 is not > 10000.

test_cases.title: Add comment at exactly MAX_COMMENT=5000 characters
steps: Open an existing ticket
Call addTicketComment(ticketId, <5000char_string>)
Verify validation: content.length > MAX_COMMENT (5000) returns false
Verify the comment is created with full 5000-character body
test_cases.expected_result: Comment is created with the full 5000-character body. Validation passes because 5000 is not > 5000.

test_cases.title: Add exactly MAX_TAGS=20 tags to ticket
steps: Create a ticket
Call updateTicket(ticketId, { tags: [<array of exactly 20 tags, each <= 100 chars>] })
Verify validation: tags.length > MAX_TAGS (20) returns false
Verify all 20 tags are stored
test_cases.expected_result: Tags array is persisted with exactly 20 entries at the maximum allowed count.

test_cases.title: Add tag at exactly MAX_TAG_LENGTH=100 characters
steps: Create a ticket
Call updateTicket(ticketId, { tags: ['a'.repeat(100)] })
Verify validation: tags.some(t => t.length > MAX_TAG_LENGTH) returns false since 100 is not > 100
Verify the tag is stored in full
test_cases.expected_result: Tag is stored with the full 100-character value.

test_cases.title: SLA policy at minimum boundary MIN_SLA_MINUTES=1
steps: Call updateSlaPolicy({ priority: 'CRITICAL', responseTimeMinutes: 1, resolveTimeMinutes: 1 })
Verify validation passes: 1 >= MIN_SLA_MINUTES (1) and 1 <= MAX_SLA_MINUTES (525600)
Create a ticket with priority CRITICAL
Verify SLA deadlines are set to exactly 1 minute (60000ms) after creation time
test_cases.expected_result: SLA policy is saved with 1-minute response and resolve times. Ticket SLA deadlines are Date.now() + 1 * 60 * 1000.

test_cases.title: SLA policy at maximum boundary MAX_SLA_MINUTES=525600 (1 year)
steps: Call updateSlaPolicy({ priority: 'LOW', responseTimeMinutes: 525600, resolveTimeMinutes: 525600 })
Verify validation passes: 525600 >= MIN_SLA_MINUTES and 525600 <= MAX_SLA_MINUTES
Create a ticket with priority LOW
Verify SLA deadlines are set to 525600 minutes from creation time
test_cases.expected_result: SLA policy is saved with 525600-minute (1 year) values. Ticket SLA deadlines are approximately 1 year from now.

test_cases.title: Create ticket with empty optional fields (null clientId, assigneeId, description, tags)
steps: Call createTicket({ title: 'Minimal ticket', status: 'OPEN', priority: 'MEDIUM', type: 'SERVICE' }) without clientId, assigneeId, description, or tags
Verify ticket is created with clientId=null, assigneeId=null, description=null, tags=[]
Verify no cross-company validation runs (validateUserInCompany/validateClientInCompany return true for undefined IDs)
test_cases.expected_result: Ticket is created with optional fields as null/empty defaults. No Inngest notification event is sent since assigneeId is null.

test_cases.title: Restore closed ticket to each of the 4 allowed statuses
steps: Create 4 tickets, close all of them
restoreTicket(ticket1.id, 'OPEN') - verify status=OPEN, slaResponseDueDate is recalculated
restoreTicket(ticket2.id, 'IN_PROGRESS') - verify status=IN_PROGRESS, slaResponseDueDate is NOT recalculated (only on OPEN)
restoreTicket(ticket3.id, 'WAITING') - verify status=WAITING
restoreTicket(ticket4.id, 'RESOLVED') - verify status=RESOLVED
Verify activity logs are created for each restore with oldValue='CLOSED' and newValue matching the target status
test_cases.expected_result: All 4 restorations succeed. validRestoreStatuses Set contains all 4. slaResponseDueDate is only recalculated when newStatus='OPEN' (per the conditional in restoreTicket). slaDueDate is recalculated for all.

test_cases.title: Reject ticket title exceeding MAX_TITLE=500 characters
steps: Call createTicket({ title: 'a'.repeat(501), status: 'OPEN', priority: 'MEDIUM', type: 'SERVICE' })
Verify the validation check: data.title.length > MAX_TITLE throws new Error('Invalid title')
test_cases.expected_result: createTicket throws Error('Invalid title') because 501 > 500. No database operation is performed.

test_cases.title: Reject description exceeding MAX_DESCRIPTION=10000 characters
steps: Call createTicket({ title: 'Test', description: 'a'.repeat(10001), status: 'OPEN', priority: 'MEDIUM', type: 'SERVICE' })
Verify the validation check: data.description.length > MAX_DESCRIPTION throws new Error('Description too long')
test_cases.expected_result: createTicket throws Error('Description too long') because 10001 > 10000.

test_cases.title: Reject comment exceeding MAX_COMMENT=5000 characters
steps: Call addTicketComment(ticketId, 'a'.repeat(5001))
Verify the validation check: content.length > MAX_COMMENT throws new Error('Invalid comment')
test_cases.expected_result: addTicketComment throws Error('Invalid comment') because 5001 > 5000.

test_cases.title: Reject more than MAX_TAGS=20 tags on ticket
steps: Call createTicket with tags array of 21 elements
Verify the validation check: data.tags.length > MAX_TAGS throws new Error('Too many tags')
test_cases.expected_result: createTicket throws Error('Too many tags') because 21 > 20.

test_cases.title: Reject tag exceeding MAX_TAG_LENGTH=100 characters
steps: Call createTicket with tags: ['a'.repeat(101)]
Verify the validation check: data.tags.some(t => t.length > MAX_TAG_LENGTH) throws new Error('Tag too long')
test_cases.expected_result: createTicket throws Error('Tag too long') because 101 > 100.

test_cases.title: Reject invalid status value not in TicketStatus enum
steps: Call createTicket({ title: 'Test', status: 'INVALID_STATUS', priority: 'MEDIUM', type: 'SERVICE' })
Verify VALID_STATUSES (Set from Object.values(TicketStatus)) does not contain 'INVALID_STATUS'
Verify throws new Error('Invalid status')
test_cases.expected_result: createTicket throws Error('Invalid status') because 'INVALID_STATUS' is not in the TicketStatus enum set {OPEN, IN_PROGRESS, WAITING, RESOLVED, CLOSED}.

test_cases.title: Reject invalid priority value not in TicketPriority enum
steps: Call createTicket({ title: 'Test', status: 'OPEN', priority: 'SUPER_HIGH', type: 'SERVICE' })
Verify VALID_PRIORITIES (Set from Object.values(TicketPriority)) does not contain 'SUPER_HIGH'
Verify throws new Error('Invalid priority')
test_cases.expected_result: createTicket throws Error('Invalid priority') because 'SUPER_HIGH' is not in the TicketPriority enum set {LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, CRITICAL}.

test_cases.title: Reject restore to CLOSED status (not in validRestoreStatuses set)
steps: Have a ticket in CLOSED status
Call restoreTicket(ticketId, 'CLOSED')
Verify validRestoreStatuses Set(['OPEN','IN_PROGRESS','WAITING','RESOLVED']) does not contain 'CLOSED'
Verify throws new Error('Invalid status')
test_cases.expected_result: restoreTicket throws Error('Invalid status') because 'CLOSED' is not in the validRestoreStatuses Set.

test_cases.title: Reject assigning ticket to user in different company
steps: Create a ticket in companyA (companyId=1)
Call updateTicket(ticketId, { assigneeId: <userId_in_companyB> })
Verify validateUserInCompany(assigneeId, companyId=1) returns false
Verify throws new Error('Invalid assignee')
test_cases.expected_result: updateTicket throws Error('Invalid assignee') because the assigneeId does not belong to the same companyId. Cross-company validation runs outside the transaction in parallel with clientId validation.

test_cases.title: Reject linking ticket to client in different company
steps: Call createTicket({ ..., clientId: <clientId_in_companyB> }) while authenticated as user in companyA
Verify validateClientInCompany(clientId, companyId) returns false
Verify throws new Error('Invalid client')
test_cases.expected_result: createTicket throws Error('Invalid client') because the clientId does not belong to the same companyId.

test_cases.title: Non-author non-admin cannot edit another user's comment
steps: Authenticate as userA, add a comment to a ticket
Authenticate as userB (role != 'admin')
Call updateTicketComment(commentId, 'hacked content')
Verify comment.userId !== userB.id AND userB.role !== 'admin'
Verify throws new Error('רק מי ששלח את ההודעה או מנהל יכול לערוך')
test_cases.expected_result: updateTicketComment throws Hebrew error message 'רק מי ששלח את ההודעה או מנהל יכול לערוך' because userB is not the author and not an admin.

test_cases.title: Non-author non-admin cannot delete another user's comment
steps: Authenticate as userA, add a comment to a ticket
Authenticate as userB (role != 'admin')
Call deleteTicketComment(commentId)
Verify comment.userId !== userB.id AND userB.role !== 'admin'
Verify throws new Error('רק מי ששלח את ההודעה או מנהל יכול למחוק')
test_cases.expected_result: deleteTicketComment throws Hebrew error message 'רק מי ששלח את ההודעה או מנהל יכול למחוק'.

test_cases.title: Reject SLA responseTimeMinutes below MIN_SLA_MINUTES=1
steps: Call updateSlaPolicy({ priority: 'MEDIUM', responseTimeMinutes: 0, resolveTimeMinutes: 60 })
Verify validation: 0 < MIN_SLA_MINUTES (1) triggers throw new Error('Invalid response time')
test_cases.expected_result: updateSlaPolicy throws Error('Invalid response time') because 0 < 1.

test_cases.title: Reject SLA responseTimeMinutes above MAX_SLA_MINUTES=525600
steps: Call updateSlaPolicy({ priority: 'MEDIUM', responseTimeMinutes: 525601, resolveTimeMinutes: 60 })
Verify validation: 525601 > MAX_SLA_MINUTES (525600) triggers throw new Error('Invalid response time')
test_cases.expected_result: updateSlaPolicy throws Error('Invalid response time') because 525601 > 525600.

test_cases.title: Ticket access scoped to companyId via getTickets WHERE clause
steps: Create a ticket in companyA (companyId=1)
Authenticate as a user in companyB (companyId=2) with canViewServiceCalls permission
Call getTickets()
Verify the Prisma query includes where: { companyId: user.companyId } which is 2
Verify the ticket from companyA is NOT in the returned items
test_cases.expected_result: Ticket from companyA is not returned because getTickets filters by companyId=user.companyId. CompanyB user cannot see companyA tickets.

test_cases.title: Reject ticket operations without canViewServiceCalls permission flag
steps: Authenticate as a user whose permissions JSON does not include canViewServiceCalls=true
Call createTicket({ ... })
Verify assertServiceAccess calls hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewServiceCalls') which returns false
Verify throws new Error('Unauthorized')
test_cases.expected_result: createTicket throws Error('Unauthorized') from assertServiceAccess because hasUserFlag(user, 'canViewServiceCalls') is false.

test_cases.title: Service mutation rate limit 30 per user per minute (prefix 'svc-mut')
steps: Authenticate as a user with canViewServiceCalls permission
Send 30 createTicket or updateTicket requests within 1 minute
Send the 31st mutation request
Verify checkActionRateLimit(String(user.id), RATE_LIMITS.serviceMutation) returns true
Verify throws new Error('Rate limit exceeded')
test_cases.expected_result: First 30 requests pass rate limiting. The 31st triggers checkActionRateLimit which increments Redis key 'rl:svc-mut:{userId}' past max=30 in windowSeconds=60, returns true, and the action throws Error('Rate limit exceeded').

test_cases.title: Service read rate limit 60 per user per minute (prefix 'svc-read')
steps: Authenticate as a user with canViewServiceCalls permission
Send 60 getTickets or getTicketDetails requests within 1 minute
Send the 61st read request
Verify checkActionRateLimit(String(user.id), RATE_LIMITS.serviceRead) returns true
Verify throws new Error('Rate limit exceeded')
test_cases.expected_result: 61st request is rate limited. Redis key 'rl:svc-read:{userId}' exceeds max=60 in windowSeconds=60.

test_cases.title: Row-level locking prevents lost updates on concurrent ticket edits
steps: Create a ticket with title='Original Title'
Simultaneously send two updateTicket requests: one setting title='Update A' and another setting title='Update B'
Verify both transactions execute SELECT FOR UPDATE via tx.$queryRawUnsafe('SELECT id FROM "Ticket" WHERE id = $1 AND "companyId" = $2 FOR UPDATE', ...)
Verify the first transaction acquires the lock, the second waits
Verify after both complete, the final title is deterministic (whichever committed last)
Verify both activity log events are fired (no lost events)
test_cases.expected_result: No lost updates occur. SELECT FOR UPDATE serializes concurrent access. Final title is either 'Update A' or 'Update B' (last commit wins). Both transactions complete without error, both Inngest events are fired.

test_cases.title: withRetry handles transient database deadlocks (code 40P01) on ticket operations
steps: Simulate a transient database error with code '40P01' (deadlock_detected) during ticket update
Verify withRetry catches the error (TRANSIENT_CODES.has('40P01') is true)
Verify it retries with exponential backoff: first retry after ~150ms, second after ~300ms
Verify the operation succeeds on retry (maxRetries=2 default)
test_cases.expected_result: withRetry transparently retries the failed operation. On deadlock (40P01), it waits baseDelayMs * 2^attempt * jitter before retrying. Max 2 retries.

test_cases.title: Row-level locking on restoreTicket prevents concurrent restore race condition
steps: Have a closed ticket
Simultaneously send two restoreTicket requests: one to 'OPEN' and one to 'IN_PROGRESS'
Verify both use SELECT FOR UPDATE in the transaction
Verify the first transaction locks the row, reads status=CLOSED, and restores
Verify the second transaction acquires the lock, but the ticket is no longer CLOSED
Note: restoreTicket does NOT check current status equals CLOSED (it just updates), so both may succeed with different final states
test_cases.expected_result: SELECT FOR UPDATE serializes access. The second restore sees the ticket after the first restore committed. Since restoreTicket does not enforce status=CLOSED precondition, both may succeed. Final status is the last to commit.

test_cases.title: Activity log records status change with Hebrew labels
steps: Create a ticket in OPEN status
Call updateTicket(ticketId, { status: 'IN_PROGRESS' })
Verify Inngest 'ticket/activity-log' event is fired with previousData.status='OPEN' and newData.status='IN_PROGRESS'
Verify the status-change fallback creates TicketActivityLog with fieldName='status', oldLabel='פתוח', newLabel='בטיפול'
test_cases.expected_result: Activity log entry is created via Inngest fire-and-forget with status change data. Hebrew status labels are used in the fallback path (statusMap: OPEN='פתוח', IN_PROGRESS='בטיפול', WAITING='ממתין', RESOLVED='טופל', CLOSED='סגור').

test_cases.title: Activity log records assignment change
steps: Create a ticket with no assignee
Call updateTicket(ticketId, { assigneeId: userA.id })
Verify Inngest 'ticket/activity-log' event contains previousData.assigneeId=null and newData.assigneeId=userA.id
Verify Inngest 'ticket/notification' event with type='assignee', isNew=false is sent
test_cases.expected_result: Activity log records the assignment change. Notification event is fired for the new assignee if assigneeId != currentUser.id.

test_cases.title: SLA breach detection when resolve time exceeded (SlaBreach record)
steps: Configure SlaPolicy with resolveTimeMinutes=1 for CRITICAL priority
Create a ticket with priority CRITICAL (slaDueDate = now + 1 minute)
Simulate time passage beyond 1 minute without resolving (e.g., via Inngest cron job or manual check)
Verify a SlaBreach record is created with breachType='RESOLVE', priority='CRITICAL', slaDueDate matching the ticket's slaDueDate
Verify the breach has status='PENDING' and companyId matching the ticket's companyId
test_cases.expected_result: SlaBreach record is created when ticket's slaDueDate passes without resolution. Breach has breachType=RESOLVE, status=PENDING. getTicketStats counts PENDING breaches.

test_cases.title: SLA recalculation on ticket restore uses current timestamp
steps: Create a ticket with CRITICAL priority (slaDueDate = T0 + 60min)
Close the ticket
Wait some time, then call restoreTicket(ticketId, 'OPEN') at time T1
Verify new slaDueDate = T1 + slaPolicy.resolveTimeMinutes * 60 * 1000
Verify new slaResponseDueDate = T1 + slaPolicy.responseTimeMinutes * 60 * 1000 (because newStatus='OPEN')
test_cases.expected_result: SLA deadlines are recalculated from Date.now() at restore time, not from the original creation time. This prevents immediate SLA breach on restored tickets.

test_cases.title: Transition ticket IN_PROGRESS -> WAITING -> IN_PROGRESS (bidirectional)
steps: Create a ticket, move to IN_PROGRESS
Call updateTicket(ticketId, { status: 'WAITING' }) - verify success
Call updateTicket(ticketId, { status: 'IN_PROGRESS' }) - verify success
Verify both transitions succeed (no status flow validation in updateTicket beyond enum check)
Verify two 'ticket/status-change' Inngest events are fired
test_cases.expected_result: Both transitions succeed. updateTicket validates the status value against TicketStatus enum but does NOT enforce a state machine transition graph. Both activity log events are created.

test_cases.title: Cursor-based pagination for large ticket list (PAGE_SIZE=100)
steps: Create 250 tickets in a single company with varying statuses (OPEN, IN_PROGRESS, WAITING, RESOLVED)
Call getTickets() with no cursor - verify 100 items returned and nextCursor is set
Call getTickets(nextCursor) - verify next 100 items and new nextCursor
Call getTickets(nextCursor2) - verify remaining 50 items and nextCursor=null
Verify each page query uses orderBy: [{ updatedAt: 'desc' }, { id: 'desc' }] and take: PAGE_SIZE + 1
Verify companyId filter is applied to each query
test_cases.expected_result: Cursor-based pagination returns PAGE_SIZE=100 items per page. The query fetches take: 101, pops the extra to determine nextCursor. All pages combined return all 250 non-CLOSED tickets.

test_cases.title: Permanently delete ticket with no related data (cascade on empty relations)
steps: Create a ticket with no comments, no tags, and no activity logs or SLA breaches
Close the ticket
Call permanentlyDeleteTicket(ticketId)
Verify prisma.ticket.deleteMany returns count=1
Verify no cascade errors occur on empty relations
